content:
# 2 Peter Chapter 1 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), page 269 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/2PE_001_page_001.jpg

### Greeting

1. This letter is from Simeon Peter, a servant and apostle of Jesus Christ. I am writing to you who share the same precious faith we have — a faith given to us through the righteousness of our God and Savior Jesus Christ.

2. May you experience more and more of God's grace and peace as you grow in your knowledge of God and Jesus our Lord.

### Growing in Spiritual Maturity

3. By his divine power, God has given us everything we need for living a godly life. We have received all of this by coming to know him, the one who called us to himself by means of his own marvelous glory and excellence.

4. And because of his glory and excellence, he has given us great and precious promises. These are the promises that enable you to share his divine nature and escape the corruption in the world caused by sinful desires.

5. In view of all this, make every effort to respond to God's promises. Add to your faith moral excellence, and to moral excellence add knowledge,

6. and to knowledge add self-control, and to self-control add patient endurance, and to patient endurance add godliness,

7. and to godliness add mutual affection among believers, and to mutual affection add love for everyone.

8. The more you grow like this, the more productive and useful you will be in your knowledge of our Lord Jesus Christ.

9. But those who fail to develop in this way are shortsighted or blind, forgetting that they have been cleansed from their old sins.

10. So, dear brothers and sisters, work hard to confirm that God has truly called and chosen you. Do these things, and you will never stumble or fall away.

11. Then God will give you a grand entrance into the eternal kingdom of our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ.

### Pay Attention to Scripture

12. Therefore, I will always remind you about these things — even though you already know them and are standing firm in the truth you have been taught.

13. And I think it is right, as long as I am in this body, to wake you up with these reminders,

14. for our Lord Jesus Christ has shown me that I must soon leave this earthly life.

15. So I will make every effort to see that after my departure you will be able to remember these things at all times.

### Eyewitnesses of Christ's Glory

16. For we were not making up clever stories when we told you about the powerful coming of our Lord Jesus Christ. We saw his majestic splendor with our own eyes

17. when he received honor and glory from God the Father. The voice from the majestic glory of God said to him, "This is my dearly loved Son, who brings me great joy."

18. We ourselves heard that voice from heaven when we were with him on the holy mountain.

19. Because of that experience, we have even greater confidence in the message proclaimed by the prophets. You must pay close attention to what they wrote, for their words are like a lamp shining in a dark place — until the Day dawns and the morning star rises in your hearts.

20. Above all, you must realize that no prophecy in Scripture ever came from the prophet's own understanding,

21. for no prophecy ever originated from human will. Instead, people spoke from God as they were carried along by the Holy Spirit.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

- **1:1 "Simeon Peter"** — Sinaiticus reads CVMEΩΝ (Symeon), the Hebraic form of the name, rather than the more common "Simon." This is one of the distinctive readings of this manuscript and is retained here as it reflects the older, Semitic form of Peter's name.
- **1:1 "our God and Savior Jesus Christ"** — The Greek construction (tou theou hemon kai soteros Iesou Christou) uses a single article governing both "God" and "Savior," applying both titles to Jesus Christ (Granville Sharp's rule). This is a significant Christological statement equating Jesus with God.
- **1:3 "his own marvelous glory and excellence"** — Sinaiticus reads idia doxa kai arete ("his own glory and excellence"), where the calling is through Christ's own divine attributes, not merely "to" glory.
- **1:4 "share his divine nature"** — The Greek theias koinonoi physeos ("partakers of the divine nature") is a striking phrase. The dynamic rendering "share his divine nature" conveys participation in God's character and life without implying absorption into deity.
- **1:5-7 The virtue chain** — The Greek uses the literary device of sorites (chain reasoning), where each virtue builds on the previous one. The dynamic translation preserves this staircase structure with "add to... add" phrasing for clarity.
- **1:9 "shortsighted or blind"** — The Sinaiticus text reads typhlos (blind), with myopazon (shortsighted) also present. The pairing suggests spiritual blindness — an inability or refusal to see the significance of one's conversion.
- **1:13 "in this body"** — Literally "in this tent/tabernacle" (skenoma). Peter uses the metaphor of the body as a temporary dwelling, echoing 2 Corinthians 5:1-4.
- **1:14 "I must soon leave this earthly life"** — Literally "the putting off of my tent is soon." This likely alludes to Jesus' prediction of Peter's death (John 21:18-19).
- **1:15 "after my departure"** — The Greek exodos ("exodus/departure") is the same word used of Jesus' death in Luke 9:31 at the Transfiguration, creating a deliberate literary connection.
- **1:17-18 The Transfiguration** — Peter appeals to his personal experience as an eyewitness of the Transfiguration (Matthew 17:1-8; Mark 9:2-8; Luke 9:28-36) to authenticate his message. The phrase "majestic glory" (megaloprepous doxes) is unique to this passage in the New Testament.
- **1:19 "morning star"** — The Greek phosphoros (light-bearer) refers to the dawn star (Venus). This is a metaphor for Christ's return or for full spiritual illumination dawning in the hearts of believers.
- **1:20-21 Prophetic inspiration** — These verses emphasize that biblical prophecy did not originate from human initiative. The prophets were "carried along" (pheromenoi) by the Holy Spirit — the same word used for a ship driven by the wind (Acts 27:15, 17) — conveying that God was the ultimate author directing the message.
